name: Java Collections Framework Hierarchy
chatResponse: I will explain the Java Collections Framework by breaking down its core interface hierarchy and showing you how different data structures relate to one another.

steps.title: The Root Architecture
steps.content: The framework is primarily divided into two main branches: the `Collection` interface (for groups of objects) and the `Map` interface (for key-value pairs). The `Collection` interface extends `Iterable`, allowing all collections to be traversed using a for-each loop.

steps.title: Ordered Lists and Indexed Access
steps.content:
The `List` interface represents an ordered sequence of elements. 
- `ArrayList`: Based on a dynamic array; best for fast random access ($O(1)$).
- `LinkedList`: Based on a doubly-linked list; best for frequent insertions and deletions ($O(1)$ at the ends).

steps.title: Sets for Uniqueness
steps.content:
The `Set` interface prevents duplicate elements. 
- `HashSet`: Uses a hash table; offers $O(1)$ performance but no guaranteed order.
- `TreeSet`: Implements `SortedSet`; stores elements in a sorted tree structure ($O(\log n)$).

steps.title: Queues and Deques
steps.content: The `Queue` interface is designed for holding elements prior to processing, typically following First-In-First-Out (FIFO). `Deque` (Double Ended Queue) allows insertion and removal from both ends, functioning as both a queue and a stack.

steps.title: The Map Interface
steps.content:
Though not a subtype of `Collection`, `Map` is a core part of the JCF. It maps unique keys to values.
- `HashMap`: Efficient, unordered storage.
- `TreeMap`: Sorted by keys using a Red-Black tree structure.

visualCode:
graph TD
    Iterable --> Collection
    Collection --> List
    Collection --> Set
    Collection --> Queue
    List --> ArrayList
    List --> LinkedList
    List --> Vector
    Set --> HashSet
    Set --> TreeSet
    Set --> LinkedHashSet
    Queue --> PriorityQueue
    Queue --> Deque
    Deque --> ArrayDeque
    Deque --> LinkedList

    Map --> HashMap
    Map --> TreeMap
    Map --> LinkedHashMap
    Map --> Hashtable
